title: Income or consumption of the richest 10%
subtitle: The level of income or consumption per person per day below which 90% of the population falls. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
note: This data is expressed in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices. Depending on the country and year, it relates to income (measured after taxes and benefits) or to consumption, [per capita](#dod:per-capita).
caption:
Income or consumption of the richest 10%

The level of income or consumption per person per day below which 90% of the population falls. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This bar chart displays the income or consumption of the richest 10% of the population for various countries in 2025, expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices. The vertical axis ranges from $0 to over $150 per person per day. The bars are arranged in descending order from left to right and are color-coded by continent: North America (orange), South America (maroon), Africa (purple), Europe (blue), Asia (teal), and Oceania (light blue). Luxembourg shows the highest value, with its bar extending well above the $150 mark, followed closely by the United States. A cluster of European nations, including Switzerland, Norway, and Iceland, occupies the next tier, with values ranging roughly between $125 and $150. The United Kingdom and Israel appear in the next group, with bars reaching just above $100. As the chart progresses to the right, the values decrease steadily. Countries like Bulgaria, Brazil, and Mexico sit in the mid-range, with bars falling between $50 and $75. Further down, China, Guatemala, and Lebanon show values between $25 and $50. The lowest values on the chart are found among African nations such as Cameroon, Burkina Faso, and the DR Congo, where the bars are very short, indicating daily amounts near or below $10. The visual distribution highlights a wide gap between the highest-income countries at the start of the chart and the lowest-income countries at the end.
